narrative_blocks.header: He moved west to Wisconsin in old age
narrative_blocks.body: Reuben Smith married Elizabeth Porter at Saratoga in 1825 and raised his family in New York. By 1860 he had moved to Beaver Dam, Wisconsin, where he died that November.

narrative_blocks.header: Husband oldest homeopathic physician on Pacific coast
narrative_blocks.body: Emily's husband Dr. Ziba Bass Nichols was described at his 1897 death as 'oldest practicing homeopathic physician on the Pacific coast' — a Dartmouth-trained physician who had worked the homeopathy revolution from its Minnesota mid-century days through to its Oregon-frontier late-century practice. He had served as physician and surgeon of the Minnesota state asylum for the deaf, dumb, and blind for eighteen years before the family's move to Portland.

narrative_blocks.header: Husband preceptor of the Mayo brothers
narrative_blocks.body: Dr. Nichols was described in a descendant biographical sketch as 'preceptor of the Mayo brothers' — placing the Porter-Nichols family in proximity to Charles Mayo and William Mayo, the founders of the Mayo Clinic. The preceptor-and-student relationship in nineteenth-century American medicine was a formal apprenticeship; if accurate, this placed Emily's household near the formative training of two of America's most consequential physicians.

Three of Emily and Dr. Nichols's ten children became Portland physicians in turn: Dr. Ammi Sibley Nichols (1854-1931), Dr. Clarence Lincoln Nichols (1861-1911), and Dr. Herbert Strong Nichols (1868-1948). The three-physician second generation extended the family's medical-practice continuity from 1844 marriage to mid-twentieth-century Oregon care.

Emily Porter was born in 1826 in Worcester, Worcester County Massachusetts, daughter of Benjamin Porter and Clementina Lamb. She died September 11, 1901 in Portland Oregon at about age seventy-five and is buried at River View Cemetery, Portland. She had been a resident of Portland eighteen years prior to her death.

naming_inspiration.description: Named 'Moses Porter' for grandfather Col. Moses Porter (HD2434).

institutions.institution_blurb: Licensed to preach in 1868 at Talcott Street Congregational Church (now Faith Congregational Church) — historic Black Congregational congregation founded 1826 in Hartford.

military_service.rank: Private (1862-64) → First Lieutenant and Brevet Captain (1864)
units: 39th United States Colored Troops (1864) — Black Union Army regiment
Aide-de-Camp to General Duncan, New Bern North Carolina (1865)
military_service.role: Enlisted Massachusetts state regiment 1862; transitioned to officer commission with one of the U.S. Colored Troops Black Union regiments in 1864. Served as aide-de-camp to General Duncan in New Bern North Carolina through war's end.

burial.plot_notes: Congressional Cemetery, Washington, District of Columbia. Cemetery linkage repair: existing person was listed in cemetery hooker_connections in prior pilots but burial.cemetery_id was null. Pilot104 closure of bidirectional reference.

narrative_blocks.body: Moses Porter Snell rose from Private in the 36th Massachusetts Volunteers (1862-1864) to First Lieutenant and Brevet Captain in the 39th United States Colored Troops in 1864 — one of the Black Union Army regiments. He served as Aide-de-Camp to General Duncan in New Bern North Carolina in 1865. Immediately postwar he became Superintendent of the Freedmen's Bureau in Washington D.C., a significant Reconstruction-era role coordinating federal support for newly emancipated African Americans.

narrative_blocks.header: Licensed to preach at Talcott Street Church, 1868
narrative_blocks.body: After ordination on May 8, 1867 from Hartford Theological Seminary, Moses Porter Snell was licensed to preach in 1868 at Talcott Street Congregational Church in Hartford — the historic Black Congregational congregation that had been founded in 1826. The Talcott Street licensing parallel between his Reconstruction-era career and his ministerial commissioning at one of New England's most prominent Black churches is striking. He spent the next forty years as missionary and pastor in Washington D.C. and Maryland-Virginia churches.

narrative_blocks.header: Grandson of Amherst College founder Thomas Snell
narrative_blocks.body: Moses Porter Snell was grandson of Dr. Thomas Snell, founder of Amherst College, and nephew of Ebenezer S. Snell (Amherst class of 1822). He himself graduated Amherst in 1868 and attended Hartford Theological Seminary 1866-1868. The two-generation Amherst College connection — founder grandfather, alumnus uncle, alumnus himself — was characteristic of the New England Congregational scholarly families that dominated late-nineteenth-century mainline Protestant institutional leadership.

narrative_blocks.header: Born North Brookfield MA, buried Congressional Cemetery DC
narrative_blocks.body: Moses Porter Snell was born May 3, 1839 in North Brookfield Massachusetts and died December 31, 1909 in Newton New Jersey at age seventy. He was buried at Congressional Cemetery in Washington D.C. He married Mary Cordelia Hallock on August 31, 1862 — the same year he enlisted in the 36th Massachusetts. They had nine children.
